journal_entry.description: Conversion of 50 preferred shares to 25 common shares
journal_entry.rationale: Share conversion is an equity-to-equity transaction. Under IFRS, when preferred shares are converted to common shares, we debit (decrease) the Preferred Shares account for the carrying value of the shares surrendered ($100) and credit (increase) the Common Shares account for the same amount. This maintains the total equity balance while reclassifying between equity accounts. No gain or loss is recognized on conversion. The carrying value of $100 for all 50 preferred shares becomes the basis for the 25 common shares issued.

ambiguities.resolution: Transaction states 'carrying value $100' — this is the book value to be used for the conversion, following standard convention for share conversions at stated amounts

ambiguities.aspect: whether conversion is per share or total
ambiguities.resolution: Context indicates '50 preferred shares (carrying value $100)' — the $100 is the total carrying value for all 50 shares being converted, not per share

ambiguities.aspect: par value of common shares issued
ambiguities.resolution: Standard convention for conversions is to record at book value of securities surrendered — the $100 carrying value of preferred shares becomes the basis for the common shares issued, regardless of par value

output.reason: This is a share conversion transaction. On the credit side, we decrease Preferred Share Capital (equity decrease) by crediting it. Under IFRS, crediting an equity account decreases its balance. The preferred shares are being removed/converted, so we credit Preferred Share Capital = equity decrease. However, equity decrease is not a credit category - it would be a debit. Let me reconsider: When converting preferred to common shares, we DEBIT preferred share capital (to decrease it) and CREDIT common share capital (to increase it). So on the CREDIT side, we are increasing Common Share Capital = equity increase (1 line).

credit_classifier.reason: This is a share conversion transaction. On the credit side, we decrease Preferred Share Capital (equity decrease) by crediting it. However, equity decrease is not a credit category - it would be a debit. Let me reconsider: When converting preferred to common shares, we DEBIT preferred share capital (to decrease it) and CREDIT common share capital (to increase it). So on the CREDIT side, we are increasing Common Share Capital = equity increase (1 line).

output.reason: Converting preferred shares to common shares involves reclassifying equity. The debit side decreases Preferred Share Capital (equity decrease) with carrying value $100. The credit side will increase Common Share Capital. This is an equity-to-equity reclassification with no impact on total equity, assets, liabilities, or income statement accounts.
